claim: At least one of seven under-tested candidate languages (Basque, Hungarian, Finnish, Armenian, Ottoman Turkish, Italian-dialect, German), when mapped to Hand A EVA glyphs by simple frequency rank, produces a decoded stream whose connector-to-content bigram rate exceeds the shuffled-baseline rate by at least +0.005 (same shuffle test as Brady/Pagel/Schechter). The top-scoring language, if any, becomes Brain-V's primary Hand-A decipherment hypothesis. If NO candidate exceeds +0.005 delta, simple frequency-rank character substitution in these languages is eliminated, pointing to cipher/constructed/shorthand options.
evidence_for: Hand A is internally natural-language-like (H-BV-HAND-A-INTERNAL-01 CONFIRMED)
Brady's mapping restricted to Hand A produced a positive (though sub-threshold) +0.0044 delta — the shuffle test has measurable discriminating power
None of the 7 candidates have been systematically tested on Hand A to date in this project

15th-century Italian/German are historically plausible by provenance; Hungarian/Finnish/Basque/Armenian/Ottoman are non-Indo-European or isolated language families that produce unusual letter-frequency profiles resembling Hand A's flat-vowel distribution
evidence_against: Simple frequency-rank substitution is the weakest possible cipher assumption and rarely survives rigorous corpora
Schechter's Latin dictionary, Brady's Syriac map, and Pagel's Latin/Hebrew/Arabic mix all failed the same shuffle test
Hand A's flat-ish glyph frequency curve (top glyph 18% vs Italian top 12%, German top 17%) suggests letter frequencies may not align well for any natural language under rank substitution
The Currier-A/B split suggests the encoding may be paradigm-specific, not language-specific — frequency substitution cannot capture that
test:
PRE-REGISTERED SEVEN-LANGUAGE SCREEN. For each candidate in [italian, german, finnish, hungarian, basque, ottoman_turkish, armenian]:

  (1) FREQUENCY TABLE: use published letter-frequency ranking for that language (sources cited in run script). Strip diacritics for mapping.
  (2) EVA TOKENIZATION: collapse {ch, sh, ckh, cth, cph} to single glyph-units as in Brain-V's standard EVA tokenizer.
  (3) FREQUENCY-RANK MAPPING: sort Hand-A EVA glyph-units by descending frequency; map rank-i EVA glyph -> rank-i language letter (top-20 positions).
  (4) DECODE: apply mapping to every Hand-A token to produce a decoded letter stream.
  (5) LEXICON MATCH: against a fixed 80-item common-word lexicon per language (top 15 function words as CONNECTORS, next 65 content words as CONTENT). Match exact or with single-letter-insertion tolerance.
  (6) SHUFFLE TEST: in-order connector-content bigram rate vs. shuffled-across-Hand-A bigram rate; delta = in-order − shuffled. Same random seed (0) and methodology as the Brady Hand-A test.

  OUTPUT: ranked list of seven deltas.
test_threshold: PRIMARY DECISION: If any language's delta >= +0.010, CONFIRMED for that language — promote to primary hypothesis. If best delta is in (+0.005, +0.010), MARGINAL for that language — promote to secondary. If best delta <= +0.005, REFUTED — simple frequency-rank substitution in all seven candidate languages is eliminated, redirecting effort to cipher/constructed/shorthand models.

tests_run.details: REFUTED. Best delta +0.00198 (german) below +0.005 threshold. All seven candidates fail. Ranked deltas: german +0.00198, finnish +0.00066, basque +0.00066, armenian -0.00066, italian -0.00099, ottoman_turkish -0.00121, hungarian -0.00638. Ottoman Turkish and Hungarian have high lexicon match rates (~11%) but NEGATIVE deltas — shuffle outperforms in-order, meaning matches are not syntactically structured. Even the best (german) sits below Brady's Hand-A +0.0044, so simple frequency-rank substitution in these seven candidates is eliminated. Points to cipher/constructed/shorthand models, not natural-language substitution.

reasoning: Hand A is natural-language-like internally, yet Brady's Syriac-mapped version is only marginally better than shuffle. Before abandoning frequency-rank substitution entirely, Brain-V screens seven untested candidate languages in a single pre-registered sweep. The negative result is as valuable as the positive: a clean REFUTED across all seven redirects effort from substitution ciphers to structural/constructed systems. The positive result identifies the next primary hypothesis.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-16 user-directed pre-registration following Brady Hand-A MARGINAL result
